You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

# Forum digest: "A few statement rows get the wrong category or payee"

Collected from a user support thread. In every case the money moved correctly;
only the derived label is wrong - the category, the sub-tag, or the counterparty
name - and only on a handful of unusual row shapes. Three representative posts,
lightly anonymized, then a maintainer note.

---

**mango_saver** -
> Two rows on this month's statement look off. A credit whose reference note is
> nothing but a long all-numeric string used to show up as a cheque deposit and
> now it just reads as a generic transfer. Separately, my salary credit - whose
> narration is a bare numeric code and nothing else - has stopped picking up the
> salary sub-tag it always had.

**rk_ontheroad** -
> Mine is the name field, not the category. On transfers whose narration opens
> with a direction word ahead of the name, the counterparty now comes back
> empty or wrong. Ordinary transfers that don't open that way are still
> labelled fine; it's only that leading-keyword narration style.

**auditlens** -
> Bill transfers that use a slash-delimited reference are pulling the wrong
> payee. When the reference is laid out as four fields - a type, a sub-type, a
> numeric reference, then the payee name as the final field - the app is lifting
> the numeric reference into the name column instead of the actual name sitting
> at the end.

---

**Maintainer note.** Consolidating these. Each one sits on an edge form that
everyday rows rarely produce, which is why routine activity looks healthy and
nothing tripped in review. On top of the three above we reproduced one more in
the same family: an offsetting credit that arrives immediately after its
matching debit is being recorded as a bounce rather than the reversal it
really is.

The labeling logic is deterministic - the same row always maps to the same
label - so each complaint can be recreated from a single synthetic row and
chased to the exact rule at fault. Expect the faults to sit on boundaries and
structural offsets inside otherwise-correct rules, not in the common paths.

**Ask for engineering:** restore correct handling so these edge rows label
correctly again, without shifting any row that already comes out right. Success
is that the situations described here enrich correctly and nothing that is
currently correct changes.

steps.message:
Analysis: Two more faults are now strongly identified. HDFC salary recognition accepts only exactly six numeric characters (`^[0-9]{6}$`), matching the numeric-only salary narration boundary complaint; this should accept longer numeric codes without affecting nonnumeric/common rows. Central's non-leading-slash bill-payment rule selects zero-based field 2, the numeric reference in a four-field `type/sub-type/reference/name` layout, instead of field 3. Together with YES's 3–6 digit cheque ceiling, these are narrow boundary/offset fixes. We still need isolate the adjacent reversal rule and leading-direction payee rule.
Plan: Run the shift diagnostic alone and inspect all resulting source blocks. Then enumerate rules where a description starts with a direction token and compare their extraction source/offset, using concise output. Once the two remaining exact lines are confirmed, patch all issues and build synthetic regression checks.

steps.message:
Analysis: The adjacent-row regression is exact in HDFC.py: the reversal rule compares the credit description to `shift(periods=2)` even though all other matching fields and the following bounce rule compare to the immediately preceding row (`shift(1)`). Consequently the reversal condition fails and the next rule labels the pair `AUTO_PAYMENT_BOUNCE`. This should be `shift(1)`. Three fixes are now identified: YES numeric cheque upper bound, HDFC numeric salary exact-length boundary, and HDFC reversal offset. The remaining leading-direction counterparty issue needs one more focused source review; likely an extraction slice/order bug in a bank's party-name rules.
Plan: Inspect transfer party-name construction in likely candidate banks, emphasizing first-pass extraction rules where `^BY`, `^TO`, or `^TRANSFER` narrations may choose a wrong structural field. Also search for suspicious direction rules using slices that skip the first field or operating on an initially empty `partyName`. Then patch all five narrow lines and test synthetic DataFrames against each bank function.

import pandas as pd
from categorizationapp.UtilityScripts.AppUtil import py_extract, sep_by_

# YES: preserve 3-6 digit handling and extend numeric-only cheque references.
remarks = pd.Series(['123', '123456', '123456789012345', '12', '123ABC'])
matched = remarks.str.contains(r'^[0-9]{3,}$', na=False, case=False)
assert matched.tolist() == [True, True, True, False, False]

# HDFC salary: preserve six digits, extend longer bare codes, reject shorter/mixed text.
desc = pd.Series(['123456', '123456789', '12345', '12345A'])
matched = desc.str.contains(r'^[0-9]{6,}$', na=False, case=False)
assert matched.tolist() == [True, True, False, False]

# HDFC reversal: the matching credit immediately follows its debit.
type_ = pd.Series(['DEBIT', 'CREDIT', 'CREDIT'])
amount = pd.Series([500, 500, 400])
desc = pd.Series(['UPI/REF', 'UPI/REF', 'UPI/REF'])
reversal = (type_.eq('CREDIT') & type_.shift(1).eq('DEBIT') &
            amount.eq(amount.shift(1)) & desc.eq(desc.shift(1)) &
            desc.str.contains(r'IMPS|NEFT|\bUPI|RTGS', na=False, case=False))
assert reversal.tolist() == [False, True, False]

# Axis: extract from the narration even when the preliminary partyName is empty.
desc = pd.Series(['TRANSFER TO ACME-42', 'ACME CORP'])
assert py_extract(desc, r'^TRANSFER TO (.*)', 0).tolist() == ['ACME-42', '']

# Central: four fields use the final payee; legacy three fields retain field 3.
desc = pd.Series(['BILLPAYMENT/UTILITY/123456/ACME POWER',
                  'BILLPAYMENT/123456/ACME POWER'])
four_field = desc.str.count('/').eq(3)
party = sep_by_(desc, 2, 3).copy()
party.loc[four_field] = sep_by_(desc, 3, 4).loc[four_field]
assert party.tolist() == ['ACME POWER', 'ACME POWER']
print('targeted regression checks passed')
PY
